summary: Implement CloudWatch Logging, Alarms, and Automated Deployment Rollback
description: Establish the full operational observability and deployment safety layer for LoanFlow 360 on AWS ECS. This epic covers configuring all six ECS services to ship structured container logs to per-service CloudWatch Log Groups, defining CloudWatch alarms for ECS service stability and ALB error rates, creating log-based metric filters that surface application-level error patterns, wiring all alarms to an SNS notification topic, and extending the Jenkinsfile ECS deploy stage with a try-catch rollback block that automatically reverts to the previous task definition revision when a deployment fails health checks. The output is a system where every log line is queryable in CloudWatch Logs Insights, every operational failure triggers a notification, and every bad deployment self-heals without manual intervention.
functionality: centralise all microservice container logs in CloudWatch, alert on service instability and elevated error rates, and automatically roll back failed ECS deployments before they reach full rollout
benefit: reduce mean time to detection for production incidents, eliminate manual rollback steps during failed deployments, and provide a queryable audit trail of every container's runtime behaviour across all environments

stories.summary: Configure CloudWatch Log Groups and awslogs driver for all ECS services
stories.description:
Create a dedicated CloudWatch Log Group for each of the six LoanFlow 360 services in each environment and configure the ECS task definitions to ship all container stdout and stderr output to those groups using the awslogs log driver. Log groups follow the naming convention /loanflow/{env}/{service-name}. Retention policies are applied to prevent unbounded log storage cost: 30 days for dev, 90 days for production. The awslogs-create-group option is enabled as a safety net but pre-created log groups with explicit retention are preferred.

Log groups to create:
- /loanflow/dev/api-gateway
- /loanflow/dev/identity-service
- /loanflow/dev/loan-service
- /loanflow/dev/document-service
- /loanflow/dev/underwriting-service
- /loanflow/dev/notification-service
- /loanflow/prod/api-gateway
- (and so on for all six services in prod)

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: All twelve log groups exist with the correct retention policy
acceptanceCriteria.then: twelve log groups are listed — six per environment — each with retentionInDays set to 30 for dev groups and 90 for prod groups

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Container stdout output appears in CloudWatch within 30 seconds
acceptanceCriteria.given: an ECS task is running with the awslogs driver configured
acceptanceCriteria.when: the Spring Boot service writes a log line to stdout
acceptanceCriteria.then: the log line appears in the corresponding CloudWatch Log Group under a log stream named after the ECS task ID within 30 seconds

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Log streams are named by ECS task ID for traceability
acceptanceCriteria.given: a task is running and has written logs
acceptanceCriteria.when: the log streams in the service log group are listed
acceptanceCriteria.then: each stream name follows the pattern {container-name}/{ecs-task-id} so logs from a specific task revision can be isolated during incident review

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Task definitions reference the correct log group for their environment
acceptanceCriteria.given: a dev task definition and a prod task definition are described
acceptanceCriteria.when: the logConfiguration awslogs-group values are compared
acceptanceCriteria.then: the dev task definition references /loanflow/dev/{service-name} and the prod task definition references /loanflow/prod/{service-name}

tasks.summary: Pre-create all twelve CloudWatch Log Groups with retention policies
tasks.description: Use aws logs create-log-group to create /loanflow/dev/{service-name} and /loanflow/prod/{service-name} for all six services. Immediately apply retention policies using aws logs put-retention-policy: 30 days for all dev groups, 90 days for all prod groups. Pre-creating groups with explicit retention prevents ECS from auto-creating groups with no retention policy (which would accumulate logs indefinitely at cost).

tasks.summary: Add awslogs logConfiguration block to all six ECS task definitions
Verify log delivery end-to-end for all six services in the dev environment
tasks.description: Force a new ECS task deployment for each dev service to trigger the updated task definition. For each service, tail the CloudWatch log group using aws logs tail /loanflow/dev/{service-name} --follow and confirm Spring Boot startup logs appear. Verify the log stream name includes the ECS task ID. Document any services where logs do not appear and check the ECS task execution role policy for logs:CreateLogStream and logs:PutLogEvents permissions.

tasks.summary: Confirm ECS task execution role has CloudWatch Logs write permissions
tasks.description: Verify the loanflow-ecs-execution-role IAM policy includes logs:CreateLogGroup, logs:CreateLogStream, and logs:PutLogEvents on the resource arn:aws:logs:*:*:log-group:/loanflow/*. The AmazonECSTaskExecutionRolePolicy managed policy includes these permissions; if a custom policy is in use, add these actions explicitly. Without these permissions, ECS tasks fail to start with a ResourceInitializationError.

stories.summary: Define CloudWatch alarms for ECS service stability and ALB error rate
stories.description: Create CloudWatch alarms that detect ECS service degradation and elevated API error rates before they escalate into full outages. Alarms cover four dimensions: ECS running task count dropping below the desired count (indicates task crashes or failed deployments), ALB 5xx error rate exceeding a threshold (indicates backend failures reaching clients), per-service CPU utilisation exceeding 80 percent (indicates resource saturation), and per-service memory utilisation exceeding 80 percent. All alarms publish to a shared SNS topic. Alarms are defined for the production environment; equivalent alarms with relaxed thresholds are created for dev.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: ECS running task count alarm fires when a service loses all tasks
acceptanceCriteria.given: the ECS task count alarm is configured for api-gateway with threshold 1
acceptanceCriteria.when: all api-gateway tasks are stopped and the running count drops to 0
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions to ALARM state within one evaluation period and publishes a notification to the SNS topic

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: ALB 5xx alarm fires when error rate exceeds 1 percent over 5 minutes
acceptanceCriteria.given: the ALB HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count alarm is configured with a sum threshold
acceptanceCriteria.when: the 5xx count exceeds the threshold across two consecutive 5-minute evaluation periods
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions to ALARM state and an SNS notification is published

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: CPU utilisation alarm fires before a service becomes unresponsive
acceptanceCriteria.given: a CPU utilisation alarm is set at 80 percent for a service
acceptanceCriteria.when: the ECS service CPU utilisation remains above 80 percent for two consecutive evaluation periods
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions to ALARM state, giving the operations team advance warning before the service starts dropping requests

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Alarms return to OK state when conditions normalise
acceptanceCriteria.given: an ECS task count alarm is in ALARM state
acceptanceCriteria.when: the ECS service recovers and the running task count returns to the desired value
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions back to OK state and an OK notification is published to the SNS topic

tasks.summary: Create ECS running task count alarms for all six production services
tasks.description: For each service in the production cluster, create a CloudWatch alarm on the ECS/ContainerInsights RunningTaskCount metric filtered by ClusterName and ServiceName. Set the threshold to less than the desired task count (e.g. < 2 for services with desiredCount 2), evaluation period 1, period 60 seconds, treat missing data as breaching. Alarm name: loanflow-prod-{service-name}-task-count-low.

tasks.summary: Create ALB 5xx error rate alarm for the production load balancer
tasks.description: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the AWS/ApplicationELB HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count metric for the loanflow-prod-alb. Use statistic Sum, period 300 seconds, threshold greater than the value representing 1 percent of expected request volume, evaluation periods 2, treat missing data as not breaching. Alarm name: loanflow-prod-alb-5xx-high. Add a second alarm on HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count to catch ALB-level errors (e.g. no healthy targets) separately.

tasks.summary: Create CPU and memory utilisation alarms for all six production services
tasks.description: For each service, create two alarms using ECS/ContainerInsights CpuUtilized and MemoryUtilized metrics normalised against CpuReserved and MemoryReserved. Set thresholds at 80 percent utilisation, evaluation periods 2, period 300 seconds. Alarm names: loanflow-prod-{service-name}-cpu-high and loanflow-prod-{service-name}-memory-high. These are early-warning alarms; they do not indicate an outage but prompt the team to investigate scaling or memory leak root causes.

tasks.summary: Create equivalent dev environment alarms with relaxed thresholds
tasks.description: Mirror the production alarm set for the dev environment with adjusted thresholds: task count threshold < 1 (since dev runs 1 task per service), ALB 5xx threshold higher to allow noisy dev testing, CPU/memory thresholds at 90 percent. Dev alarms publish to the same SNS topic but use a distinct alarm name prefix loanflow-dev- so notifications can be filtered or routed differently.

stories.summary: Configure CloudWatch log metric filters for application-level error patterns
stories.description: Create CloudWatch Logs metric filters that parse container log output for application-level error patterns — specifically ERROR and EXCEPTION log levels emitted by the Spring Boot services — and publish a count metric to a custom CloudWatch namespace. Alarms on these metrics fire when error volume crosses a threshold, catching application bugs that manifest as exceptions rather than HTTP 5xx codes. A CloudWatch Logs Insights saved query is created for each service to accelerate incident investigation by surfacing the most recent error stack traces in a single query.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Metric filter counts ERROR log lines and publishes to a custom namespace
acceptanceCriteria.given: a metric filter is applied to the /loanflow/prod/loan-service log group
acceptanceCriteria.when: the loan-service logs an ERROR-level line
acceptanceCriteria.then: the LoanFlow360/ApplicationErrors metric in the custom namespace increments by 1 within the next CloudWatch metric flush interval

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Application error alarm fires when error count exceeds threshold in 5 minutes
acceptanceCriteria.given: a CloudWatch alarm monitors the ApplicationErrors metric for a service
acceptanceCriteria.when: more than 10 ERROR-level log lines appear in a 5-minute window
acceptanceCriteria.then: the alarm transitions to ALARM state and publishes a notification to the SNS topic

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Logs Insights saved query returns the most recent exception stack traces
acceptanceCriteria.given: a Logs Insights saved query is defined for a service
acceptanceCriteria.when: the query is run for the last 1 hour
acceptanceCriteria.then: the results show the most recent ERROR and EXCEPTION log events with their full message field, sorted by timestamp descending

tasks.summary: Create ERROR-level log metric filters for all six production log groups
tasks.description: For each /loanflow/prod/{service-name} log group, create a metric filter with filter pattern [level=ERROR] or { $.level = "ERROR" } depending on the log format. Map each filter to a CloudWatch metric in the custom namespace LoanFlow360/ApplicationErrors with metric name {service-name}-error-count, value 1, unit Count. This requires the log output to be in a consistent format; confirm Spring Boot is configured to output structured JSON logs or a parseable pattern.

tasks.summary: Configure Spring Boot structured JSON logging for all six services
tasks.description: Add the spring-boot-starter-json logging dependency or configure logback-spring.xml in each service to output logs in JSON format with fields: timestamp, level, service, traceId, message. JSON-formatted logs allow CloudWatch metric filters to use JSON filter syntax ({ $.level = "ERROR" }) which is more reliable than regex-based pattern matching against unstructured text. Apply to all six services under <backend/{service-name}/src/main/resources/logback-spring.xml>.

tasks.summary: Create application error count alarms and CloudWatch Logs Insights saved queries
tasks.description:
For each service, create a CloudWatch alarm on the LoanFlow360/ApplicationErrors {service-name}-error-count metric with threshold > 10 over a 5-minute period, evaluation periods 1, treat missing data as not breaching. Create a CloudWatch Logs Insights saved query per service named LoanFlow360 - {service-name} - Recent Errors with query:
```
fields @timestamp, level, message, @logStream
| filter level = "ERROR" or level = "WARN"
| sort @timestamp desc
| limit 50
```

stories.summary: Provision SNS notification topic and operational alert routing
stories.description: Create an SNS topic that receives all CloudWatch alarm state change notifications for the LoanFlow 360 production and dev environments. Configure subscriptions to route alert notifications to the operations team. A separate SNS topic is created per environment so dev noise can be filtered or routed to a lower-priority channel. All alarms defined in the monitoring stories reference these topics as their alarm actions. A test notification is triggered for each topic to confirm end-to-end delivery before the system goes live.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: CloudWatch alarm state change publishes a message to the SNS topic
acceptanceCriteria.given: an SNS topic is configured as the alarm action for a CloudWatch alarm
acceptanceCriteria.when: the alarm transitions to ALARM state
acceptanceCriteria.then: an SNS message is published to the topic within 60 seconds of the state transition

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: SNS topic subscription receives and delivers the notification
acceptanceCriteria.given: an email or endpoint subscription is confirmed on the SNS topic
acceptanceCriteria.when: a message is published to the topic
acceptanceCriteria.then: the subscriber receives the notification with the alarm name, state, metric value, and a direct link to the CloudWatch alarm

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Dev and production alarm notifications are routed to separate topics
acceptanceCriteria.given: separate SNS topics exist for dev and production
acceptanceCriteria.when: a dev alarm fires and a prod alarm fires simultaneously
acceptanceCriteria.then: the dev notification is delivered only to the dev topic subscribers and the prod notification only to the prod topic subscribers

tasks.summary: Create loanflow-prod-alerts and loanflow-dev-alerts SNS topics
tasks.description: Create two SNS topics: loanflow-prod-alerts and loanflow-dev-alerts with encryption enabled using the default AWS-managed KMS key (aws/sns). Tag both topics with Project=loanflow-360 and Environment=prod or dev. Record the topic ARNs — they are referenced as the AlarmActions, OKActions, and InsufficientDataActions values in all CloudWatch alarm definitions.

tasks.summary: Configure SNS subscriptions for the operations team
tasks.description: Add email subscriptions for the operations team to the loanflow-prod-alerts topic. Confirm each subscription by clicking the confirmation link sent to the subscriber email. For dev, add subscriptions to loanflow-dev-alerts for developers who want to monitor dev environment stability. If a Slack webhook is available, add an HTTPS subscription pointing to the Slack incoming webhook URL for loanflow-prod-alerts.

tasks.summary: Update all CloudWatch alarm definitions to reference the correct SNS topic ARN
tasks.description: For every alarm created in the ECS stability, ALB error rate, CPU/memory, and application error stories, set AlarmActions and OKActions to the appropriate topic ARN: loanflow-prod-alerts for production alarms and loanflow-dev-alerts for dev alarms. This can be applied in bulk using aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm with the --alarm-actions flag or by updating the infrastructure-as-code definitions.

tasks.summary: Trigger a test notification to validate end-to-end SNS delivery
tasks.description: Use aws sns publish --topic-arn <loanflow-prod-alerts-arn> --message 'LoanFlow 360 SNS test notification' --subject 'Test Alert' to confirm delivery to all subscribers. Also trigger a real alarm state change by manually setting an alarm to ALARM state using aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state and confirming the formatted alarm notification arrives at all subscription endpoints.

stories.summary: Implement automated rollback in Jenkinsfile on ECS deployment failure
stories.description: Extend the ECS Deploy stage in the root Jenkinsfile to detect deployment failures and automatically execute a rollback to the previous task definition revision. The current implementation calls aws ecs update-service and aws ecs wait services-stable but has no failure handling. This story adds a try-catch block around the stability wait for each service, retrieves the prior task definition ARN on failure, re-issues update-service with the previous revision to restore the last known good state, and posts the rollback outcome to the GitHub commit status. The build is marked FAILED regardless of whether the rollback succeeds, ensuring the failed deployment is visible in the pipeline history.

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Deployment failure triggers automatic rollback to the previous task definition
acceptanceCriteria.given: a new task definition revision fails ALB health checks and does not reach stable state within the wait timeout
acceptanceCriteria.when: aws ecs wait services-stable times out in the Jenkinsfile catch block
acceptanceCriteria.then: the Jenkinsfile retrieves the previous task definition ARN and calls update-service with it, restoring the last healthy revision

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Build is marked FAILED after rollback regardless of rollback success
acceptanceCriteria.given: a deployment failure triggers the rollback path
acceptanceCriteria.then: the Jenkinsfile calls error() to mark the build as FAILED and the Jenkins pipeline stage turns red, even if the rollback itself succeeded

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: GitHub commit status reflects the deployment failure with a link to the build
acceptanceCriteria.given: the Jenkinsfile posts GitHub commit status updates
acceptanceCriteria.when: the ECS deploy stage enters the catch block
acceptanceCriteria.then: the GitHub commit shows a red failure status with the description 'ECS deployment failed — rollback initiated' and a URL linking to the Jenkins build log

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Successful deployments are unaffected by the rollback logic
acceptanceCriteria.given: a new task definition revision passes health checks and reaches stable state
acceptanceCriteria.when: aws ecs wait services-stable returns without error
acceptanceCriteria.then: the try block completes normally, the catch block is not entered, and the build continues to the next stage

acceptanceCriteria.scenario: Rollback failure is logged and does not mask the original deployment failure
acceptanceCriteria.given: the rollback update-service call itself fails
acceptanceCriteria.when: the catch block within the catch block handles the nested failure
acceptanceCriteria.then: both the original deployment failure and the rollback failure are logged to the Jenkins console and the build is still marked FAILED

tasks.summary: Wrap each ECS update-service and wait block in a try-catch in the Jenkinsfile
tasks.description:
For each per-service ECS deploy block in the Jenkinsfile, wrap the sequence:
```
sh 'aws ecs update-service --cluster ${ECS_CLUSTER} --service <name> --force-new-deployment'
sh 'aws ecs wait services-stable --cluster ${ECS_CLUSTER} --services <name>'
```
in a try block. The catch block receives the exception and proceeds to rollback logic. This change is applied to all six service deploy blocks — each service has its own independent try-catch so a failure in one service does not prevent rollback attempts for others.

tasks.summary: Implement previous task definition ARN retrieval in the catch block
tasks.description:
In the catch block, retrieve the current (failing) task definition ARN by running:
```
aws ecs describe-services --cluster ${ECS_CLUSTER} --services <name> \
  --query 'services[0].taskDefinition' --output text
```
Parse the revision number from the ARN (format: arn:aws:ecs:region:account:task-definition/name:revision). Decrement the revision number by 1 to derive the previous ARN. Store both as Groovy variables for use in the rollback step and the failure log message.

tasks.summary: Implement rollback update-service call and stability wait in the catch block
tasks.description:
After retrieving the previous task definition ARN, execute the rollback:
```
sh "aws ecs update-service --cluster ${ECS_CLUSTER} --service <name> \
  --task-definition ${previousTaskDefArn}"
sh "aws ecs wait services-stable --cluster ${ECS_CLUSTER} --services <name>"
```
Wrap this inner sequence in its own try-catch. If the rollback stability wait also fails, log 'ROLLBACK FAILED for <service> — manual intervention required' to the console. In both cases, proceed to call error() to mark the build as FAILED.

tasks.summary: Post deployment failure and rollback status to GitHub commit status
tasks.description:
In the catch block, after the rollback attempt, use the GitHub Steps plugin to post a failure commit status:
- context: 'ECS Deploy / {service-name}'
- state: 'failure'
- description: 'Deployment failed — rolled back to revision {previousRevision}' (or 'Deployment failed — rollback also failed' if the rollback step threw)
- targetUrl: the Jenkins build URL from env.BUILD_URL
This gives engineers a direct link from the failing GitHub commit to the full Jenkins console log without navigating through the Jenkins UI.

tasks.summary: Add deployment history summary to Jenkinsfile post block
tasks.description: Add a post { always { } } block to the ECS Deploy stage that echoes a deployment summary table to the console: service name, deployed revision (or rolled-back revision), cluster, and final outcome (DEPLOYED / ROLLED BACK / FAILED). This summary is the first thing an engineer sees when opening the build log and avoids having to scroll through the full log to determine which services deployed successfully and which did not.
